About Me
Hannah Jeongseo Kim
Political science and psychology student- Pre-Law
Click to upload photo
  • UniversityUniversity of Cincinnati
  • YearSophomore, Class of 2029
  • MajorsPolitical Science & Psychology
  • MinorGerman
  • ProgramUniversity Honors Program
  • In SeoulMay–December 2026
  • LanguagesEnglish, Korean, German
  • CareerLaw / Diplomacy / Human Rights

Who Am I?

I'm a second-year honors student double majoring in political science and psychology, pursuing a career of law combined with international diplomacy. I am passionate about understanding interational institutions and their impact on human rights. I am interested in researching the impacts authoritarian regimes have on developing democracies. 

I presented my paper at the UC Annual Symposium on South Korean democratic resistance and radical hope, received the Tolley International Human Rights Scholarship, and am a Cincinnatus Century Scholar. 

Academic Timeline

  • Fall 2025 – Spring 2026
    Freshman year at UC. Presents at Annual Symposium. Awarded Tolley Scholarship. Nominated for KU exchange.
  • May 2026
    Departs for Seoul. Settling in to new city.
  • June – August 2026
    KU Korean Language Center (KLC) Regular Program. Full-time coursework with UC
  • September – December 2026
    KU Fall Exchange Semester. Political science, psychology, and German coursework
  • Spring 2027+
    Returns to UC. Explore pre-law organizations and begin studying for LSAT. Continue research and explore co-ops, internships, and study abroad opportunities.

Research Interests

Korean Democratic ResistanceRadical Hope TheoryHuman Rights LawPolitical PsychologyKorean CultureUS-Korea RelationsComparative DemocratizationInternational InstitutionsPre-LawGerman Studies
Academic Research
Research & Scholarship
Undergraduate research at the intersection of political science, Korean studies, and behavioral psychology.
Featured Paper · UC Annual Symposium 2026
South Korean Democratic Resistance and the Politics of Radical Hope

"This paper will examine radical hope in South Korean society as an intergenerational political movement. I hope to reveal the imperfections of South Korea and celebrate the resistance of the citizens who demand democracy. Democratic resistance is inherited and is a valuable tool for justice..."- From the Abstract


Radical HopeKorean DemocracyPolitical PhilosophyComparative Politics1980s Democratization

Presented at the UC Annual Symposium, "Radical Hope"

Future Research Directions
Developing Research Avenues

Political Psychology & Identity

How immigrants and foreigners navigate their cultural and political identities in civic participation.

Mental Health in East Asian Cultures

Analyzing the stigma surrounding mental health conversations among the east asian community.

Democratic Backsliding

Expanding the Radical Hope paper into a comparative framework applicable to other post-authoritarian societies.

Psychology of Protest

What motivates collective action and how to retain the flame of "radical hope" among today's youth and civic society.

Human Rights & International Law

Frameworks regarding human rights, international relations, and international law with an emphasis on North Korean and South Korean relations.

Comparative European Democracy

Utilizing Germany as a comparative case to post-authoritarian democratization- planning a Germany study abroad experience in the future

Journal
The Blog
Reflections, observations, and honest writing from seven months in Korea.
